<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md, @kconfig.md

</global_review_guidelines>


Target Commit:
commit 1d0ff7ee0c57c0adf1f8543e4613ddaa22321ab3
Author: Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>
Date:   Mon Mar 23 11:12:13 2026 +0000

    mm: kmemleak: add CONFIG_DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_VERBOSE build option
    
    Add a Kconfig option to default kmemleak verbose mode on at build time.
    This option depends on DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_AUTO_SCAN since verbose reporting
    is only meaningful when the automatic scanning thread is running.
    
    When enabled, kmemleak prints full details (backtrace, hex dump, address)
    of unreferenced objects to dmesg as they are detected during scanning,
    removing the need to manually read /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak.
    
    Making this a compile-time option rather than a boot parameter allows
    debug kernel flavors to enable verbose kmemleak reporting by default
    without requiring changes to boot arguments. A machine can simply swap
    to a debug kernel and benefit from kmemleak reporting automatically.
    
    By surfacing leak reports directly in dmesg, they are automatically
    forwarded through any kernel logging infrastructure and can be easily
    captured by log aggregation tooling, making it practical to monitor
    memory leaks across large fleets.
    
    The verbose setting can still be toggled at runtime via
    /sys/module/kmemleak/parameters/verbose.
    
    Signed-off-by: Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

diff --git a/mm/Kconfig.debug b/mm/Kconfig.debug
index 7638d75b27db..91b3e027b753 100644
--- a/mm/Kconfig.debug
+++ b/mm/Kconfig.debug
@@ -297,6 +297,17 @@ config DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_AUTO_SCAN
 
 	  If unsure, say Y.
 
+config DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_VERBOSE
+	bool "Default kmemleak to verbose mode"
+	depends on DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_AUTO_SCAN
+	help
+	  Say Y here to have kmemleak print unreferenced object details
+	  (backtrace, hex dump, address) to dmesg when new memory leaks are
+	  detected during automatic scanning. This can also be toggled at
+	  runtime via /sys/module/kmemleak/parameters/verbose.
+
+	  If unsure, say N.
+
 config PER_VMA_LOCK_STATS
 	bool "Statistics for per-vma locks"
 	depends on PER_VMA_LOCK
diff --git a/mm/kmemleak.c b/mm/kmemleak.c
index fa8201e23222..2eff0d6b622b 100644
--- a/mm/kmemleak.c
+++ b/mm/kmemleak.c
@@ -241,7 +241,7 @@ static int kmemleak_skip_disable;
 /* If there are leaks that can be reported */
 static bool kmemleak_found_leaks;
 
-static bool kmemleak_verbose;
+static bool kmemleak_verbose = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_VERBOSE);
 module_param_named(verbose, kmemleak_verbose, bool, 0600);
 
 static void kmemleak_disable(void);

    "content": "{"content":"Kernel Memory Leak Detector\n===========================\n\nKmemleak provides a way of detecting possible kernel memory leaks in a\nway similar to a `tracing garbage collector\n<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tracing_garbage_collection>`_,\nwith the difference that the orphan objects are not freed but only\nreported via /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak. A similar method is used by the\nValgrind tool (``memcheck --leak-check``) to detect the memory leaks in\nuser-space applications.\n\nUsage\n-----\n\nCONFIG_DEBUG_KMEMLEAK in \"Kernel hacking\" has to be enabled. A kernel\nthread scans the memory every 10 minutes (by default) and prints the\nnumber of new unreferenced objects found. If the ``debugfs`` isn't already\nmounted, mount with::\n\n  # mount -t debugfs nodev /sys/kernel/debug/\n\nTo display the details of all the possible scanned memory leaks::\n\n  # cat /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak\n\nTo trigger an intermediate memory scan::\n\n  # echo scan > /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak\n\nTo clear the list of all current possible memory leaks::\n\n  # echo clear > /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak\n\nNew leaks will then come up upon reading ``/sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak``\nagain.\n\nNote that the orphan objects are listed in the order they were allocated\nand one object at the beginning of the list may cause other subsequent\nobjects to be reported as orphan.\n\nMemory scanning parameters can be modified at run-time by writing to the\n``/sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak`` file. The following parameters are supported:\n\n- off\n    disable kmemleak (irreversible)\n- stack=on\n    enable the task stacks scanning (default)\n- stack=off\n    disable the tasks stacks scanning\n- scan=on\n    start the automatic memory scanning thread (default)\n- scan=off\n    stop the automatic memory scanning thread\n- scan=<secs>\n    set the automatic memory scanning period in seconds\n    (default 600, 0 to stop the automatic scanning)\n- scan\n    trigger a memory scan\n- clear\n    clear list of current memory leak suspects, done by\n    marking all current reported unreferenced objects grey,\n    or free all kmemleak objects if kmemleak has been disabled.\n- dump=<addr>\n    dump information about the object found at <addr>\n\nKmemleak can also be disabled at boot-time by passing ``kmemleak=off`` on\nthe kernel command line.\n\nMemory may be allocated or freed before kmemleak is initialised and\nthese actions are stored in an early log buffer. If\n     a pointer to a white object is found, the object is added to the\n     gray list\n  3. scan the gray objects for matching addresses (some white objects\n     can become gray and added at the end of the gray list) until the\n     gray set is finished\n  4. the remaining white objects are considered orphan and reported via\n     /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak\n\nSome allocated memory blocks have pointers stored in the kernel's\ninternal data structures and they cannot be detected as orphans. The task stacks also increase the\namount of false negatives and their scanning is not enabled by default.\n\nThe false positives are objects wrongly reported as being memory leaks\n(orphan). Okay, here's my assessment of this patch. It's a straightforward modification to `mm/kmemleak.c`. The patch simply changes the initialization of the `kmemleak_verbose` static variable to use `IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_VERBOSE)` and introduces the corresponding Kconfig option `DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_VERBOSE`.

Let's break this down systematically, as requested.

*   **Task 1: Callee Traversal:** This patch doesn't modify any callee functions. It only affects the initialization of a static variable.

*   **Task 2: Caller Traversal:** The primary use of `kmemleak_verbose` is within the `kmemleak_scan` function.  It's used within a conditional check: `if (kmemleak_verbose) print_unreferenced(...)`.  The `print_unreferenced` function works on the current object with its object lock held, so there's no semantic change here.

*   **Task 3: Lock Requirements:** No new locks are introduced or removed. The existing locking scheme within `kmemleak_scan` remains unchanged.

*   **Task 4: Error Paths:** There are no changes to error paths with this patch.

*   **Task 5: Resource Propagation:**  No new resources are added or modified.

*   **Task 5B: RCU Ordering:** No changes related to RCU.

*   **Task 6: Loop Control:** No changes to loop control are introduced.

*   **Task 7: Initialization:**  The patch introduces an initialization for a static variable.  That is `kmemleak_verbose = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DEBUG_KMEMLEAK_VERBOSE);` which is handled correctly.

The\n *   object_list is the main list holding the metadata (struct\n *   kmemleak_object) for the allocated memory blocks. The object trees are\n *   red black trees used to look-up metadata based on a pointer to the\n *   corresponding memory block. The kmemleak_object structures are added to\n *   the object_list and the object tree root in the create_object() function\n *   called from the kmemleak_alloc{,_phys,_percpu}() callback and removed in\n *   delete_object() called from the kmemleak_free{,_phys,_percpu}() callback\n * - kmemleak_object.lock (raw_spinlock_t): protects a kmemleak_object.\n *   Accesses to the metadata (e.g. count) are protected by this lock. Note\n *   that some members of this structure may be protected by other means\n *   (atomic or kmemleak_lock). This lock is also held when scanning the\n *   corresponding memory block to avoid the kernel freeing it via the\n *   kmemleak_free() callback. This is less heavyweight than holding a global\n *   lock like kmemleak_lock during scanning.\n * - scan_mutex (mutex): ensures that only one thread may scan the memory for\n *   unreferenced objects at a time. The gray_list contains the objects which\n *   are already referenced or marked as false positives and need to be\n *   scanned. This list is only modified during a scanning episode when the\n *   scan_mutex is held. At the end of a scan, the gray_list is always empty.\n *   Note that the kmemleak_object.use_count is incremented when an object is\n *   added to the gray_list and therefore cannot be freed. This mutex also\n *   prevents multiple users of the \"kmemleak\" debugfs file together with\n *   modifications to the memory scanning parameters including the scan_thread\n *   pointer\n *\n * Locks and mutexes are acquired/nested in the following order:\n *\n *   scan_mutex [-> object->lock] -> kmemleak_lock -> other_object->lock (SINGLE_DEPTH_NESTING)\n *\n * No kmemleak_lock and object->lock nesting is allowed outside scan_mutex\n * regions.\n *","end_line":50,"lines_read":50,"path":"mm/kmemleak.c","start_line":1,"total_lines":2344}]}",
